sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In a strict (full) binary tree every internal node has exactly two children. If I is the number of internal nodes and L the number of leaves, L=I+1. Hence the total N=I+L=2L−1, which is always odd. In particular, an odd L still gives an odd N.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: N cannot be even. It may equal the number of leaves only in the one-node boundary case, so C is not universally justified by the stated condition. Also N=2L−1 is less than, not greater than, 2L.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: A full binary tree satisfies L=I+1 and N=2L−1.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A complete graph has one edge for every unordered pair of distinct vertices. The number of such pairs is C(n,2)=n(n−1)/2.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other expressions do not count unordered pairs: n counts only linearly, n(n+1)/2 includes extra self-like terms, and n²/2 is not generally even an integer.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Edges of Kₙ correspond one-to-one with 2-element vertex subsets.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The last car to enter is positioned nearest the exit end of the accessible chain and must leave before cars parked earlier behind it can leave. Thus entry and removal follow last-in, first-out order, exactly the behavior of a stack.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A queue is FIFO, while array and linked list describe storage layouts rather than the access discipline emphasized by the question.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: When the most recently inserted item must be removed first, the structure is a stack.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Deleting the first node of a singly linked list requires only changing the head pointer (and freeing the old node), so it is Θ(1), not Θ(n). Therefore statement B is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Searching an unsorted list and finding a maximum require a full Θ(n) traversal. Finding/deleting an unspecified middle node also requires Θ(n) traversal unless a direct pointer is supplied.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Head insertion/deletion is constant time; position/value discovery generally requires traversal.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Insert in order: 37→2, 38→3, 72 hashes to 2 and probes to 4, 48→6, and 98→0. Key 11 hashes to 4, finds it occupied by 72, and moves by linear probing to index 5. Thus 11 is stored at 5.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Indices 3 and 4 are already occupied when 11 arrives, while index 6 is not reached because index 5 is the first free probe position.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Linear probing checks h(k),h(k)+1,… cyclically until the first empty slot.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A gateway connects dissimilar or separately administered networks and forwards/translates traffic between them, allowing a device on one network to communicate with another network.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A hub or ordinary switch connects devices within a local network segment. Utility and file servers provide services but are not the general inter-network forwarding device.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Inter-network communication requires a layer-3 gateway/router function.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.answerText: Cannot be determined from the question — window size depends on sequence-number bits and protocol variant
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: There is no protocol-independent numeric maximum. With an m-bit sequence field, Go-Back-N normally permits at most 2^m−1 outstanding frames, while Selective Repeat normally limits the window to 2^(m−1) to avoid ambiguity. The stem supplies neither m nor the sliding-window variant, so 4, 8, 15, and 16 cannot be selected uniquely. For example, m=4 would give 15 for Go-Back-N but 8 for Selective Repeat.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Each listed number becomes plausible only after adding an unstated sequence-space size and protocol assumption.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Window limits are derived from the finite sequence-number space and retransmission protocol.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Ethernet, Frame Relay, and Token Ring define data-link framing/MAC behavior. HSSI is a high-speed serial physical interface, and 10BASE-T primarily specifies an Ethernet physical-layer medium/signaling implementation. Thus the intended data-link-only set is 1,3,5.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options containing item 2 or 4 mix physical-layer interface/media standards into the data-link set.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Separate framing and media-access protocols from physical connectors, signaling, and cabling variants.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A large bus/tree cable can suffer unequal attenuation and signal levels over distance. Dividing it into shorter segments connected by repeaters limits each electrical span and regenerates the signal, overcoming the balancing problem. This is segmentation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Polling controls access, modulation changes signal representation, and merely using a stronger transmitter does not correctly regenerate and isolate long cable sections.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Segment long shared media so repeaters can restore signal quality between spans.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Token Ring access is deterministic (ii-a). A cut-through switch begins forwarding before receiving the whole frame and can exploit full wire speed/low latency (iii-b). Spanning Tree prevents switching loops (iv-c). The remaining pairing is Ethernet with valid-address-code checking as worded (i-d). Therefore i-d, ii-a, iii-b, iv-c.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The alternatives assign determinism to Ethernet or exchange the established cut-through and spanning-tree functions.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Token passing is deterministic; cut-through forwards early; spanning tree removes loops.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: An absolute loader places object code at the addresses already prescribed in the object program. Address adjustment/relocation decisions must therefore be resolved before loading; in the standard scheme the assembler produces the absolute addresses. Option A's 're-allocation' is evidently the paper's wording for relocation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The loader itself performs the physical loading. In the classic absolute scheme, allocation and intermodule linking are typically assigned by the programmer, whereas the assembler translates/relocates address-dependent references into absolute form.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: An absolute loader does no run-time relocation; it obeys addresses prepared earlier.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Grammar A describes nested strings whose leading production choice is recoverable from the eventual handle/lookahead, and grammar B describes a^n c followed by n symbols from {a,b}; both have deterministic rightmost reductions with one-symbol lookahead and are LR(1). Grammar C, A→A+A|a, is ambiguous because a+a+a has two parse trees. Hence both A and B, but not C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Options A and B omit the other LR(1) grammar. Option C selects an ambiguous grammar, and an ambiguous grammar cannot be LR(k).
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: LR grammars must be unambiguous; canonical LR(1) lookahead can resolve deterministic nested handles.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The intended third production is W→Sz (the scan resembles 'S2'). Reduce the input xxxxyzz bottom-up: y→S prints 2; Sz→W prints 3; xxW→S prints 1; the next Sz→W prints 3; and the outer xxW→S prints 1. The translation is 2 3 1 3 1.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: The other sequences print actions in top-down derivation order or reorder reductions. A shift-reduce parser executes semantic actions when each handle is reduced, from the innermost phrase outward.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For bottom-up parsing, record actions in reduction order, not production-expansion order.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A pushdown automaton recognizes exactly the context-free languages. A two-way linear-bounded automaton is at least as powerful for this purpose and can simulate the necessary stack within linearly bounded tape. Therefore both B and C can recognize CFG languages.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A finite automaton recognizes only regular languages, a strict subset of CFLs, so A alone is insufficient.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: PDA = CFL recognition; stronger bounded-tape machines can also recognize CFLs.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A synthesized attribute at a parent is computed from attributes of its children. Bottom-up LR parsing completes/reduces the children before reducing their parent, so these values can be stored on the parse stack and combined naturally at reduction time.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Top-down LL parsing reaches a parent before its children are complete and is more naturally associated with inherited information. Ambiguity is unrelated to attribute evaluation.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Synthesized values flow upward, matching bottom-up reductions.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Round-robin keeps ready processes in FIFO order, but adds a time quantum: when a running process exhausts its quantum, it is preempted and placed at the rear. It is therefore the preemptive form of FIFO/FCFS scheduling.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: FILO, shortest-seek-first, and longest-time-first do not describe the circular ready-queue discipline.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Round-robin = FIFO rotation plus timer-driven preemption.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A page fault occurs when a process references a virtual page that is not currently resident in physical memory. The operating system must locate/load it (or reject the access if invalid) before the instruction can continue.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: A page fault is not inherently a programming error, nor is it defined by the previous reference or the vague occurrence descriptions in the other choices.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Valid virtual address + absent resident page triggers a page fault.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: In the conventional semaphore interpretation, a negative value records how many wait operations could not proceed. If s=−n, its magnitude n is the number of processes blocked in the semaphore's waiting queue.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: n+1 and n−1 introduce unsupported offsets, and zero would contradict the negative count.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: For a counting semaphore, a negative magnitude represents queued waiters.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Best-fit allocation searches the free holes and chooses the smallest hole large enough for the request. This leaves the least immediate unused remainder in the selected hole.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: First-fit uses the first sufficient hole; worst-fit uses the largest; buddy allocation chooses power-of-two blocks through splitting/coalescing.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Best fit means the smallest adequate free block.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The Unix wc utility reports counts of lines, words, and bytes/characters; wc -c gives bytes and wc -m gives characters in implementations supporting it. Thus wc is the listed command used for the required count.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: nc is commonly netcat, while chcnt and lc are not the standard Unix character-count utility.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Use wc with the appropriate count option, especially -m for characters.
